data.adaptation_notes: Bako is identified as a high potential maize production site with favourable climate and extended rainfall seasons. The region is suitable for maize cultivation and has been used for DSSAT-CERES-Maize model calibration and evaluation. Uniform intra/inter row spacing of 25 cm x 75 cm was adopted, giving a plant population of 53,333 plants/ha. Di-ammonium phosphate (DAP) rate was adopted for Bako stations. In Morogoro and Wami basin, Tanza­ nia, 40 kg/ha of nitrogen was incorporated during sowing and a similar amount for top dressing (Kadigi et al., 2020). Keywords that were used to search the databases include ‘crop modelling’, ‘climate impacts’, ‘maize’, ‘DSSAT’, ‘CERES Maize’ and ‘East Africa’. Excessive rainfall leads to maize yield loss of a comparable mag­ nitude to extreme drought in the united States. The model simulates the devel­ opment and growth of maize on a daily basis from planting until physiological maturity (Lana et al., 2018).
data.normal_planting_window: I., Stordal, F., Benestad, R., Mourice, S., Pereira-Flores, ons, whereas approaches such as varying sowing dates are recommended for semi-humid to humid zones.

location_contexts.evidence: BH-660 was calibrated and evaluated at Bako National Maize Research Center, Ethiopia, which is a high potential maize production site with favourable climate and extended rainfall seasons. Uniform intra/inter row spacing of 25 cm x 75 cm was adopted, giving a plant population of 53,333 plants/ha. Di-ammonium phosphate (DAP) rate was adopted for Bako stations.
The study was conducted in five EA countries where maize is largely grown, including Ethiopia. Bako is among the sites with high potential for maize production and favourable climate.

Keywords that were used to search the databases include ‘crop modelling’, ‘climate impacts’, ‘maize’, ‘DSSAT’, ‘CERES Maize’ and ‘East Africa’. Excessive rainfall leads to maize yield loss of a comparable mag­ nitude to extreme drought in the united States. data.adaptation_notes: BH540 was calibrated and evaluated for Hawassa and Ziway, Ethiopia, in a region with climate ranging from cool sub-humid to humid agroecologies with extended rainfall seasons. High maize production potential was observed in these regions under baseline climate. All cultivars were simulated with uniform management: 25 cm x 75 cm spacing, 53,333 plants/ha, 10 cm planting depth, and region-specific nitrogen rates. Adaptation practices such as tied ridges, micro-dosing, mulching, and small-scale irrigation are recommended for drier areas, but not specifically for Hawassa. In Morogoro and Wami basin, Tanza­ nia, 40 kg/ha of nitrogen was incorporated during sowing and a similar amount for top dressing (Kadigi et al., 2020). Keywords that were used to search the databases include ‘crop modelling’, ‘climate impacts’, ‘maize’, ‘DSSAT’, ‘CERES Maize’ and ‘East Africa’. Excessive rainfall leads to maize yield loss of a comparable mag­ nitude to extreme drought in the united States. The model simulates the devel­ opment and growth of maize on a daily basis from planting until physiological maturity (Lana et al., 2018).
data.normal_planting_window: Sowing date for Hawassa: 15 June (Abera et al., 2018).
data.planting_density: 25 cm x 75 cm spacing; 53,333 plants/ha; 10 cm planting depth.

data.adaptation_notes: Katumani is located in a semi-arid zone in Kenya and is expected to experience a maize production decline of -25% to -30% under an extreme temperature rise of +3°C and a 30% decline in precipitation. Increased precipitation can compensate for yield losses from elevated temperatures, with a potential yield gain of 16% under increased precipitation and warming. Site-specific soil, water, and land management adaptation strategies are recommended, including soil and water conservation for dry regions and varying sowing dates for semi-humid to humid zones. Nutrient enhancement and cultivar variation are also suggested as feasible adaptation strategies. For the nitrogen fertilization application, the recom­ mended amounts in the published studies were adopted. Maize Yield in Different Sites Under the Baseline Period Maize yield was stimulated for the study sites under the synthetic climate change scenarios. The results show that warm­ ing accompanied by a decrease in precipitation might favour the highland areas, whereas the drought-prone regions might be severely affected. The calibration process determines the cultivar coefficients that govern the accumulation of temperature heat units and plant development from emergence to physiological matu­ rity (Jones et al., 2003).
data.normal_planting_window: Strategies for soil and water conservation are recommended for dry regions, whereas approaches such as varying sowing dates are recommended for semi-humid to humid zones.

location_contexts.evidence: The analysis points to adverse effects in the semi-arid zones, with maize production in Katumani (Kenya)... expected to decline by -25% to -30% under an extreme temperature rise of +3°C and a 30% decline in precipitation. The results also reveal that increased precipitation will compensate for yield losses resulting from elevated temperatures in both arid and humid zones. The potential yield gain under increased precipitation and warming is 16%... in Katumani...

data.adaptation_notes: Bako is identified as a high potential maize production site with favourable climate and extended rainfall seasons. The region is suitable for maize cultivation and has been used for DSSAT-CERES-Maize model calibration and evaluation. Uniform intra/inter row spacing of 25 cm x 75 cm was adopted, giving a plant population of 53,333 plants/ha. Di-ammonium phosphate (DAP) rate was adopted for Bako stations. In Morogoro and Wami basin, Tanza­ nia, 40 kg/ha of nitrogen was incorporated during sowing and a similar amount for top dressing (Kadigi et al., 2020). Keywords that were used to search the databases include ‘crop modelling’, ‘climate impacts’, ‘maize’, ‘DSSAT’, ‘CERES Maize’ and ‘East Africa’. Excessive rainfall leads to maize yield loss of a comparable mag­ nitude to extreme drought in the united States. The model simulates the devel­ opment and growth of maize on a daily basis from planting until physiological maturity (Lana et al., 2018).

data.adaptation_notes: BH540 was calibrated and evaluated for Hawassa and Ziway, Ethiopia, in a region with climate ranging from cool sub-humid to humid agroecologies with extended rainfall seasons. High maize production potential was observed in these regions under baseline climate. All cultivars were simulated with uniform management: 25 cm x 75 cm spacing, 53,333 plants/ha, 10 cm planting depth, and region-specific nitrogen rates. Adaptation practices such as tied ridges, micro-dosing, mulching, and small-scale irrigation are recommended for drier areas, but not specifically for Hawassa. In Morogoro and Wami basin, Tanza­ nia, 40 kg/ha of nitrogen was incorporated during sowing and a similar amount for top dressing (Kadigi et al., 2020). Keywords that were used to search the databases include ‘crop modelling’, ‘climate impacts’, ‘maize’, ‘DSSAT’, ‘CERES Maize’ and ‘East Africa’. Excessive rainfall leads to maize yield loss of a comparable mag­ nitude to extreme drought in the united States. The model simulates the devel­ opment and growth of maize on a daily basis from planting until physiological maturity (Lana et al., 2018).
data.normal_planting_window: Sowing date for Hawassa: 15 June (Abera et al., 2018).

location_contexts.location_name: study area (East Africa, including Katumani, Kenya)
location_contexts.management_context: locally adopted cultivars, site-specific adaptation strategies recommended
location_contexts.evidence: We used calibrated cultivar coefficients of locally adopted varieties in twelve sites across the region. Consequently, we generated synthetic scenarios of precipitation and temperature changes... to characterize the impact of climate change on maize production across the region.

data.adaptation_notes: Katumani is located in a semi-arid zone in Kenya and is expected to experience a maize production decline of -25% to -30% under an extreme temperature rise of +3°C and a 30% decline in precipitation. Increased precipitation can compensate for yield losses from elevated temperatures, with a potential yield gain of 16% under increased precipitation and warming. Site-specific soil, water, and land management adaptation strategies are recommended, including soil and water conservation for dry regions and varying sowing dates for semi-humid to humid zones. Nutrient enhancement and cultivar variation are also suggested as feasible adaptation strategies. For the nitrogen fertilization application, the recom­ mended amounts in the published studies were adopted. Maize Yield in Different Sites Under the Baseline Period Maize yield was stimulated for the study sites under the synthetic climate change scenarios. The results show that warm­ ing accompanied by a decrease in precipitation might favour the highland areas, whereas the drought-prone regions might be severely affected. The calibration process determines the cultivar coefficients that govern the accumulation of temperature heat units and plant development from emergence to physiological matu­ rity (Jones et al., 2003).
data.normal_planting_window: Strategies for soil and water conservation are recommended for dry regions, whereas approaches such as varying sowing dates are recommended for semi-humid to humid zones.

data.adaptation_notes: The effect of nitrogen-fertilizer and optimal plant population on the profitability of maize plots in the Wami river sub-basin, Tanzania: A bio-economic simulation approach. An integrated crop model and GIS decision support system for assisting agronomic decision making under climate change. Excessive rainfall leads to maize yield loss of a comparable mag­ nitude to extreme drought in the united States. The calibration process determines the cultivar coefficients that govern the accumulation of temperature heat units and plant development from emergence to physiological matu­ rity (Jones et al., 2003).

data.adaptation_notes: In Morogoro and Wami basin, Tanza­ nia, 40 kg/ha of nitrogen was incorporated during sowing and a similar amount for top dressing (Kadigi et al., 2020). Keywords that were used to search the databases include ‘crop modelling’, ‘climate impacts’, ‘maize’, ‘DSSAT’, ‘CERES Maize’ and ‘East Africa’. Excessive rainfall leads to maize yield loss of a comparable mag­ nitude to extreme drought in the united States. The model simulates the devel­ opment and growth of maize on a daily basis from planting until physiological maturity (Lana et al., 2018).
